title: Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita, 2024
Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of workers in informal employment and GDP per capita for nations in 2024, with the horizontal axis representing GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale and the vertical axis showing the percentage of informal employment. Circles sized by population represent individual countries, color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data displays a clear negative association where higher GDP per capita coincides with lower shares of informal employment. At the lower end of the economic scale, countries such as the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Chad, and Somalia cluster with informal employment shares between 80% and 90%. India appears as a large circle in the upper-middle section, indicating a high population and an informal employment share near 90% at a GDP per capita around $10,000. Moving toward the middle of the distribution, nations like Indonesia, Egypt, and Thailand show informal employment rates between 60% and 80%. In the upper-right quadrant, countries with GDP per capita exceeding $50,000, including Germany, Spain, and South Korea, consistently show informal employment shares below 20%. Russia and Turkey occupy intermediate positions with shares around 20% to 30%. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time. Employment is defined in line with the 13th ICLS and includes the production of goods for own use, such as subsistence farming. GDP per capita is expressed in international-$ at 2021 prices. Not all deaths in a country may have been registered with a cause of death. The visible pattern shows a consistent decline in informal employment shares as GDP per capita increases across the plotted range.
